Transcrição Completa: Building a 4.7 Million Business in one Year with Organic Growth with Jesse Rogers   [00:00]  "Appreciate it, guys. Cool. So, today, um, we're just going to be talking about how I built a $4.7 million business in one year through organic growth alone."  [00:12]  "Now, I know again you guys are probably thinking like, please run ads. We're going to start running ads, but we're going to focus on how we did it in the last year organically." [00:22]  "Um, now we did run a little bit of ads. Um, in the last year, again, we generated $4.7 million in revenue. How much do you guys think that we spent on ads to get that $4.7 million?" [00:35]  "$500, $500,000, $500, little more than that. Any other guesses? 10K. So you say 10K. Cool." [00:48]  "So let's go and break it down."   [00:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Revenue Breakdown"   NÚMEROS:  $4.7 million total revenue   VISUAL:  Demonstração de receita total através de processador de cartão de crédito, pagamentos alternativos como crypto, interface do sistema WOP's   [01:05]  "So here's just the um totals and revenue through credit card processor um alternate payments like crypto, all that good stuff. And then we just switched to WOP's pretty cool. Um pretty cool thing if you ever want to look into that if you're running any info or any kind of membership stuff."   [01:22]  "Now, let's take a look at the ads."   [01:24] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "YouTube Ads Spend"   NÚMEROS:  $6,167 total spend, 2-3 sales generated   VISUAL:  Painel de gastos do YouTube Ads mostrando período de um ano desde o lançamento do negócio   [01:28]  "So, this is YouTube ads. Uh you can see the date range. You actually probably can't see it. It's very small, but that's over the last uh year when we since we launched the business. So, we only spent $6,167. And most of that was really, you know, just audience building. I think we generated two or three sales off that. Nothing nothing major."   [01:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Facebook Ads Spend"   NÚMEROS:  $5,822 total spend   VISUAL:  Painel de gastos do Facebook Ads   [01:52]  "Now, for Facebook ads, spent $5,822. So again, nothing major. Haven't really spent much on ads."   [02:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Organic Leads Generation"   NÚMEROS:  5,000-6,000 organic leads per month (exemplo de 1 mês mostrado)   VISUAL:  Dashboard de leads mostrando volume mensal de leads orgânicos, conversão para appointments qualificados   [02:05]  "So what that adds up to um or actually this in the meantime of this, we have still been generating a ton of organic leads. So this is just from 1 month. Usually it's around 5,000 to 6,000 organic leads per month. Of course, not all are qualified. Um but once they get to the appointment count, those are qualified appointments. Uh triaged by a setter."   [02:28]  "So without paid ads, still generating a ton of leads, a ton of appointments. And we're going to break down exactly how I do that today."   [02:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Total Investment vs Revenue"   NÚMEROS:  $11,989 total ad spend, $4.7M revenue, <1% chargeback/refund rate   VISUAL:  Comparação visual entre investimento total em ads e receita gerada, destaque para taxa de chargeback   [02:38]  "But that adds up to a total of $11,900 in total ad spend to generate $4.7 million in revenue. Not too bad if you ask me. And then that is also with less than a percent chargeback or refund rate, which I think is pretty important, especially in the info and trading business."   [02:58]  "As Jeremy was saying, um there's a lot of it's it's a challenging industry. There's a lot of skepticism. Uh it's hard to get a high success rate because how many of you guys have tried trading since it's kind of went around in this mastermind? It's hard, right? Sucks."   [03:18]  "As Jeremy said, it's a lot of emotionally. He said, what is it? The trauma house. And I can feel that cuz I've been trading for 8 years. Uh many of those years were not successful."   [03:32]  "So, it it's a challenging thing to sell at a high ticket and then have somebody, you know, go through everything and and see success. It's cuz it's really it's a little different than ads. You have to really hold them accountable to do all the things a lot of stuff that you know if you guys are running ads agencies you guys are working with a total different type of person to get them success."   [03:55]  "So I just added that in because it's pretty uh pretty impressive in my opinion in the trading industry or info at all."   [04:03]  "So how do we do it? Well mainly it was through YouTube."   [04:07] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "YouTube Growth Timeline"   NÚMEROS:  Janeiro 2023: 2,600 subscribers → Hoje: 177,000 subscribers, 70% dos leads via YouTube   VISUAL:  Gráfico de crescimento mostrando evolução de subscribers ao longo de 1 ano e 9 meses, comparação com outras plataformas (Instagram, Telegram)   [04:10]  "Now I have seen some growth on other channels of course um kind of just as like a drip down but as you can see I I don't know if you can see cuz this is way smaller than I envisioned when I was making this presentation. We were at 2300 or 2600 subscribers in January of 2023."   [04:30]  "So was that a year and 9 months? Year and 9 months ago we were at 2600 subscribers and today we are at 177,000. So that is pretty much how we are generating all the leads through Instagram. 70% give or take and um that that's we have some of the leads coming through um Instagram and Telegram and stuff like that but it's YouTube is the cash cow."   [04:55]  "Now just a couple years ago though I was walking door to door."   [04:59] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Background Story - Door to Door Sales"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Imagem representativa de vendas porta a porta, jornada de vendedor a closer   [05:02]  "So a little bit about myself I won't give you my whole life story um but uh I used to be in sales I started out walking door to door and went through that whole industry a little bit I ended up being a closer um I won't bore you all with that."   [05:18]  "But in just a couple of years walking around in the rain doing door-to-door sales um I realized that you know I didn't want to do that. You know I didn't want to stay doing door-to-door sales. I never would have thought that I'd be sitting here talking to a bunch of awesome people that you know some of you guys are making over a million dollars a month. A lot of people in here are absolutely killing it."   [05:42]  "So one thing though that I did learn that was really valuable when I was doing door-to-door sales is this is an app that we used to use. I can't even remember the name of it. I think it was can't remember what it was, but all of those are different houses that you had to knock on."   [05:57] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Door to Door Sales App"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Interface de app mostrando mapa com múltiplas casas marcadas para visitação   [06:00]  "Has anybody in here ever done door to door sales? Fucking sucks, right? Absolutely terrible. Um, and why is it terrible?"   [06:10]  "People answer the door, say no. Exactly. Or slamming the door in your face just like I would most of the time in a in a nice way to most door to door people."   [06:20]  "There's no leverage. It's one to one on your time. Yeah. Yeah. You're walking around basically get told to fuck off all day and making no money while doing it. Not not a great not a great endeavor."   [06:34]  "So um but you know with door-to-door it's actually quite similar to YouTube at first. How many of you guys have a YouTube channel or have started one? I know Mace kills it on YouTube. Bunch of you guys have YouTubes."   [06:48]  "So whenever you started out how many views were your videos getting and how long did it take you to make that video?"   [06:55]  "An hour right? Or or sometime you know you put you put thought into it. there's more than just like the and then you post it and you see, you know, 77 views and you're probably like, 'Fuck this.' Right?"   [07:08]  "It's kind of similar to door to door, right? If you just walk around doing door to door all the time, you just get a bunch of doors closing your face. YouTube is pretty similar when you start."   [07:18]  "I think that's why most people, especially a lot of you guys that are making good money, you don't really need to have YouTube. You don't really need to do it. So, whenever you go out and try to do it, or if you ever have tried to do it, it's pretty discouraging because it doesn't give you like an instant ROI."   [07:35]  "You're just basically getting a door slammed in your face countless times and you finally just say, 'Well, fuck this. I don't need this.'"   [07:43]  "But I think that no matter what, whether you guys are, you know, doing ads or whatever it may be, having an organic presence can help a ton. So, today I'm going to show you how I did it and how I think any of you guys can do it, too."   [07:57]  "Whether you're an agency, whether you're a coach, whether you're doing anything online, you need to have some kind of organic presence and have a structure to doing it so you can do it in a way that's not going to suck up all of your time."   [08:11]  "Because a lot of you guys are already busy doing whatever the thing that you're doing is now. Yes, because I know everybody and Jeremy has been getting on me for not running ads. We are starting to scale ads."   [08:23]  "So, of course, you will still have a paid element in your business, but the organic element can supplement that in a ton of ways."   [08:31]  "And if you're thinking, you know, for a lot of you guys who run agencies, well, you know, people aren't going to I'm not going to find clients. You know, if you guys are an agency, you think, 'Okay, well, what is YouTube going to do for me?'"   [08:45]  "Because people that are, you know, trying to, you know, use your services, they're not going to be looking up how to launch an ad campaign cuz they're going to be looking for someone to pay, right? That's probably what a lot of you guys that are running ads agencies are thinking, sitting here saying like, 'Well, this doesn't really matter to me.'"   [09:04]  "Well, I think that's not true because no matter what, having a big presence online is going to build credibility. For example, the way that I even found Jeremy was through a mutual friend. I hadn't heard of him yet cuz I'm not in this digital marketer realm."   [09:20]  "So, what was the first thing I went and did? I went and looked him up on social media. And now, fast forward less than a year, I'm here speaking at his event. And at some point in the future, we're likely to work together on different projects in some way, shape, or form."   [09:37]  "So, you see how those things can be a nest egg and compound later on. And it just takes one really good relationship to make all the time you put into YouTube worth it because if you do it correctly, it actually doesn't suck up all your time like you're probably thinking it's going to."   [09:54]  "It's not going to be um just, you know, a failed attempt. And this is all the objections I get from people when they say they don't want to start a YouTube. Like it does it doesn't it's it's not worth my time. I'm already I have this project I'm focusing on."   [10:09]  "But today, we're going to show you how you can do it in a measurable way that's very easy that quite literally any of you can do. So it gives you credibility and authority."   [10:19] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "The 4P Flywheel System"   NÚMEROS:  4 componentes principais   VISUAL:  Diagrama circular mostrando o conceito de flywheel com 4 P's interconectados   [10:22]  "Now, I tried to come up with um you know, just an overall system of how I did it because when Jeremy came and asked me to do this talk, I was thinking I was like, what's going to really be valuable for these people from YouTube?"   [10:35]  "So, I broke this down into something I call the 4P flywheel, right? Cuz content is like a flywheel. How many of you guys understand what a flywheel is or how it works?"   [10:45]  "So, for those of you who don't, you know that little merry-go-round thing at a playground you push or anybody is there one out there? I don't know. No, but you know that little thing you like spin around the roundabouts."   [11:00]  "So the harder you spin it, the faster it goes and then you know you get to a point where you don't even have to push anymore. You're barely just keeping it going. And that's kind of how having organic content will work for your business."   [11:13]  "Because if you understand how to build the right presence and build a platform, then it's going to filter into your product. It's going to filter into the people you work with and it all just starts working in synergy. And it's actually rather simple."   [11:28]  "I think a lot of people think that it's way harder than it actually is."   [11:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "First P - PRESENCE"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Ênfase na construção de presença autêntica, contraste entre highlight reels vs autenticidade   [11:35]  "So, the first thing I want to talk about is just building presence because everybody's trying to be a content creator now. Everybody's trying to start a YouTube or start a TikTok or whatever it may be. And most of the people do the exact same thing."   [11:51]  "That's how you get to the, you know, here's three tips on how to start your agency or here's three things of that nature and nobody wants to see that. So, it's actually not that hard to stand out from that kind of stuff."   [12:05]  "So, whenever I started out making content, I had been trading for years. I had been in the sales space, not in the kind of info or online sales world. I used to do solar sales. Um, I had been in the trucking industry, a lot of different businesses. And I didn't really understand the content game."   [12:25]  "But I think this played as a benefit to me because I wasn't looking at what everybody else was doing. I wasn't looking up how to make, you know, create content. I just started doing it."   [12:36]  "And I think that that's why it grew so fast because, you know, 2,300 to 177K in a little over a year, it's pretty fast, right? Especially with no paid, you know, nothing filtering into it."   [12:50]  "Um, but I firmly believe the reason that I did that or I was able to do that is just because of the presence that I had because in a world where everybody showcases all highlight reels, like that's all you see on Instagram, that's all you see on YouTube is a bunch of flashy stuff, a bunch of just the same shit over and over again, right?"   [13:11]  "And nobody wants to watch that. But if you're just authentic, if you just talk about what you actually do and do it live, if you're doing something worth a fuck people are going to want to watch it."   [13:23]  "Somebody, maybe it's not your ideal client, but there is somebody out there who is going to want to watch that. And if you consistently do it enough, you will be able to build an audience while doing this."   [13:35]  "And that's why I like to build in public because it's not really about perfection. And it's not about creating this polished, you know, studio style content because I think that's why a lot of people don't do it."   [13:48]  "They think that it's going to be hard. They think that they're going to have to hire a whole team. They're going to have to do all of these different, you know, have to get all these lights, put all these cords around their office and um you don't have to do all that because I simply just started sharing every step of the way."   [14:08]  "And that's how you actually build a brand that's going to be able to sell because you have a lot of people who have really big brands or really big presences online. I talk to these people all the time. They can't sell worth the fuck because they don't have any connection to the people that are watching their content."   [14:25]  "So, you just want to think about what kind of content would you want to watch when you're starting out doing whatever it is that you're doing. So, you guys most of you guys are running agencies. That's the vibe that I've have gotten from this mastermind."   [14:40]  "Coaching. Coaching. So, most of you guys are doing coaching. Agencies. Agencies and coaching."   [14:47]  "So, well, for coaching, it's really I think it's easy. It's just like, what are you teaching people to do? Go do it live. If you're running an agency, you can also do that live. Do whatever you're doing on a day-to-day, like onboarding a client. Here's me closing a big deal. Here's, you know, these kinds of things."   [15:07]  "There's there's endless amount of ideas you can do. I started an agency with zero dollars to prove that it was real. It's like what Cam did. You know, there's tons of stuff you you've done some pretty cool like the copywriting course that you had."   [15:22]  "There's there's tons of stuff that you can create that people are going to want to watch and it's something that you would have wanted to have when you started."   [15:30]  "So like for me, for example, for trading, it's really easy because everybody wants to teach how to trade and they'll draw lines on a chart and do all this, but why don't you just trade? Just go live and just trade. That's what I did."   [15:44]  "I did that every single day and it grew rapidly because it's not that hard to stand out when you just do things like that."   [15:52] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Early Content Examples"   NÚMEROS:  Videos com ~100 views cada, Janeiro 2023, 2,600 subscribers   VISUAL:  Thumbnails de vídeos antigos mostrando qualidade básica, alguns sem thumbnails profissionais, métricas baixas de visualização   [15:55]  "So, that's another question I get is like what type of content to start. So, I always say to build in public. This is me. Um, you can see these thumbnails are terrible. Half of them don't even have thumbnails. Um, this is at the beginning of last year."   [16:12]  "And these videos have like 100 views. Kind of similar to probably the same point that a lot of you guys are at. But at the end of the day, authenticity is going to trump perfection every single time."   [16:24]  "And you want to share all of your successes and your failures because when you're able to do that, when you're able to actually go out and just not be this polished highlight reel, people are going to be drawn to that a lot more than if you're just taking, you know, every single precaution you can take to not show any kind of downside to what you're doing."   [16:45]  "That's what a lot of people end up doing is they go out and they try to just create this perfect image and then they just never start and they never start filling any content."   [16:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Three Pillars of Marketing"   NÚMEROS:  3 pilares   VISUAL:  Diagrama mostrando Awareness, Nurture, e Trust como pilares fundamentais do marketing   [16:58]  "Now, another thing with content is, you know, the three pillars of marketing. What is that? That book? It's like the hawk method is I don't know. You guys are way more advanced in a lot of the marketing. So, you guys are probably sitting here thinking this guy."   [17:13]  "But it's awareness, nurture, and trust. That's really what you're trying to do with people, right? And you can do all of these things very easily and very effectively through organic content if you are doing things with authenticity."   [17:28]  "And being authentic is a lot less work. It's a lot less time than you have to spend in trying to make this polished type of content. Which is why a lot of people don't want to make content is they think they have to do it in that way."   [17:42]  "But making content in that overproduced way is why people get stuck. Why they end up hiring some agency and then they have a thousand followers for 6 months and never grow."   [17:53]  "Now, you'll get a lot better in the process too at whatever you're doing. Like for me, for example, for trading, um for those of you who trade, you understand this. It's not it's not fun to trade in front of a bunch of people and then not do good, you know?"   [18:08]  "It's great whenever you do great. Everyone's like, 'Woo, this guy's a fucking beast.' If you lose, you're like, 'Fuck you, scammer.' You know what I mean? Like that's that's how it always is."   [18:18]  "But it makes you a lot better at whatever it is you do. So whether it's trading or anything else, that still remains the same."   [18:26]  "So, one thing to just remember whenever you're trying to make content for those of you who walk away and actually try to start doing YouTube is that in a world of highlight reels and all this bullshit authenticity is going to be your superpower and you'll attract the right kind of people and build an audience much faster doing that than trying to invest a ton of money and time into trying to be a Hollywood creator is what I like to call it."   [18:52] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Second P - PLATFORM (YouTube Strategy)"   NÚMEROS:  $40,000 recebido do YouTube em revenue share   VISUAL:  Comparação entre investimento em ads ($11,989) vs receita do YouTube ($40,000), conceito de "inverse marketing"   [18:55]  "So, now let's talk about the platform. And for this, we're going to talk about YouTube. But you can take this same exact strategy, the same way of thinking, and apply it to any form of content, whether it's copywriting, email, Instagram, talking live, whatever it is. It's all the same."   [19:12]  "Now, remember earlier we talked about paying for ads, right? I I've only paid $11,000 for any advertising, but really, I haven't paid anything because YouTube's given me $40,000. So, it's actually been like inverse marketing, right?"   [19:28]  "So, I just thought that'd be pretty cool to add in. That's why I like um one of the reasons I like YouTube."   [19:34] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "5 Key Platform Components"   NÚMEROS:  5 segmentos   VISUAL:  Lista dos 5 elementos: Linking, Structuring, Voice, Storytelling, Finding Topics   [19:37]  "So, we're going to break this down into five segments. Number one is going to be linking. We're going to go to structuring, how to use your voice, storytelling, which is probably the most important, and then how to find topics, cuz that's where most people screw up with YouTube."   [19:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "LINKING - Capturing Attention"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Conceito de linking como ferramenta de captura de atenção, conexões indiretas entre ideias não relacionadas   [19:58]  "So, what is linking? Well, linking is just a way that you're going to capture people's attention because that's the number one hardest thing to do when you're creating content. Whether you're creating a short uh whether you're creating a 30 minute long YouTube video, whatever you're doing, um the number one thing you have to focus on is just capturing their attention."   [20:20]  "That's first and foremost because if you can't do that, it's kind of like if a big tree falls in the woods and nobody's around to hear it, did it really fall? Right? Doesn't matter how good your content is if people aren't watching it."   [20:33]  "And it's also going to enhance memorability because especially on short form um platforms, but really YouTube as well, you can make videos and people will watch them and not even remember who you are."   [20:45]  "But if you drop in little things throughout the video that will trigger a memory from them later on when they're thinking about doing whatever the thing is they're trying to learn from you, you're going to be a lot more memorable and there's a higher probability they're going to come back to your content."   [21:02]  "And the longer that somebody spends watching your content, the more likely they are to be bought in on whatever the thing is that you're selling them, right? Or whatever the the service you're doing or whatever brand you're trying to build."   [21:14]  "It also is going to build a lot of relatability if you do it correctly. Now, there's a wrong way and a right way to use this concept."   [21:22]  "So, really what it is is just making indirect uh connections between seemingly unrelated ideas. You can use this to connect any story to any situation. And we're going to talk about this with the storytelling stuff later."   [21:36]  "Um Jeremy actually did an amazing example of what I'm about to talk about um at the beginning of his talk."   [21:43] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Linking Example: Icebergs = Teams"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Comparação visual entre características de icebergs e dinâmicas de equipe, palavras associadas a cada conceito   [21:46]  "So let's just say icebergs are just like teams, right? Makes no sense, right? At first you're going to sit here and say that that has, you know, there's no way. Um but what you do and you can do this with any two things."   [22:00]  "Now, this is just an off-the-wall example um to show you you could do this with literally anything, but you can link this back to really any story you had, whether it's from childhood, creating your product, whatever problem you're solving, um your journey to learning whatever the thing is you do, you can link that to about anything."   [22:20]  "So, what you're going to do is you just take words from both thing or you know, just take words that are associated with both of the words that you're talking about. So with icebergs, right, they're hidden, massive, cold. Most of them are unseen."   [22:35]  "Now with teams, you're going to think of things like, you know, the cliche stuff, collaboration, trust, foundation, communication, all that good stuff."   [22:44]  "So let's just say you are trying to make a piece of content and you're trying to get somebody's attention early on. You could say just something off the wall, you know, like icebergs or team dynamics are a lot like icebergs."   [22:58]  "What you see on the surface, which is communication, collaboration, is just a fraction of how it actually works, right? And you can segue into this and you know, obviously this is a kind of off-the-wall metaphor, but you can do this with really anything that you want to do it with, but the challenge is just getting the stories together."   [23:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "STRUCTURING - Video Framework"   NÚMEROS:  99% de canais falham por falta de estruturação adequada   VISUAL:  Framework de estruturação de vídeo, comparação entre abordagem comum vs abordagem efetiva   [23:22]  "So, let's talk about structuring. Now, how many of you guys have ever watched a YouTube video where you're just watching it for so long and you're just like, 'Dude, I wish this guy would just fucking tell me what he's going to talk about, right?'"   [23:35]  "Like, all the really big YouTubers are really good at doing that. You just watch it forever and you're just like, 'Fuck, give me the tell me the thing.' That's how what structuring is going to do."   [23:45]  "And 99% of channels fail because they don't understand how to do this. And they just get right into the content. They just start off here's three things on here. Here's what I'm going to teach you in the video. They just go right into the content of the video and they don't give any reason to actually watch the video."   [24:03]  "They don't hook people in. They don't get people um if for to draw it to marketing, they don't build any intent throughout the process. Sure, you might get somebody to click, but you don't build any intent whatsoever if you don't use this correctly."   [24:18]  "So, it's going to hook viewers instantly. It's going to keep them watching longer. And it's going to help you connect with your audience because that is also extremely important whenever you're trying to create content."   [24:30]  "If you don't connect, you're just a body in a chair that's talking. Here's another talking head video like everybody is seen every single day when they get on social media."   [24:40]  "And on top of all that, it makes content creation a lot easier because I think the reason that most of you guys don't do content is cuz most you most of you guys make a lot of money. You don't really fucking need to, right? That's that's probably the reason. And it is a lot of work."   [24:56]  "But if you can make it as easy as possible, you're actually much more likely to do it. And it can pay huge dividends if you can just get over that hump of thinking that it's a waste of time or not giving a fuck about it enough to do it. So that's that's my goal is just to make it easy for you guys."   [25:13]  "Now, a good rule of thumb with structuring is you always want to leave people hungry. Now, a lot of you guys are marketers. You already know this. It's kind of like the um the soap opera thing, right?"   [25:25]  "You want to keep everybody like why are soap operas popular, right? I mean, I have no fucking idea. I think they're ridiculous, but the reason people watch them is because they always keep people paying attention. They always keep people wondering what's going to happen next, even though they're completely stupid."   [25:41]  "So, you always want to just leave people hungry."   [25:44] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Video Structure Framework"   NÚMEROS:  5 componentes estruturais   VISUAL:  Framework mostrando: Friend (Rapport) → Why (Importance) → What (Core Value) → How (Application) → Friend (Close/CTA)   [25:47]  "So, there is a very simple structure that I follow with all of the YouTube videos and you can use it with talks, you can use it with short form, whatever."   [25:56]  "Um, just you want to always open with rapport and I want to just call this the friend. Okay, we're going to come back to the friend archetype. We talk about voices later on. So, this is where you just build connection. You tell stories."   [26:10]  "Like, for example, I talked about um you know, being a door to door salesman. You could just talk about little things that aren't really, you know, about the thing you're talking about. Just build some rapport, right? Kind of break the ice."   [26:24]  "Now, after that, you want to explain the importance. Now, this is the part of the video where you're watching and you're just wanting the person to get to the point, but you're still watching, right?"   [26:36]  "As long as you can continuously build up and build up and build up and build up till the climax of when you're actually talking about the thing, cuz as soon as you get into the thing, you've given the person what they came to the video for, and they have no reason to stay."   [26:51]  "But if you're able to just continuously build intrigue, build up their desire for whatever the thing is, they're more likely to watch the video. They're more likely to click on whatever link you want them to click on or subscribe or whatever the desired outcome is. So the why is the most important in my opinion."   [27:09]  "Now with the what, this is pretty easy. This is where you're just delivering the core value. And all of these different uh words like there's the friend, the why, the what. These are all different voice archetypes that we're going to go over just in a second."   [27:24]  "Now, the most uh neglected thing that a lot of people do is provide practical application. So, I always like to say there's tangible and intangible value in content."   [27:36]  "Like, how many pieces of content have you ever watched and it's just completely useless? It's just telling you about like work harder or like be disciplined, you know, or like oh yeah, like get more leads or you know, it's like a lot of like Hormozi style content where you watch and you're just like, okay, great. Now, what the fuck do I do? You know what I mean?"   [27:57]  "That's like all fucking content creation pretty much. So, very easy thing to do is actually tell people tangible steps on how the fuck to do it and do it for free."   [28:08]  "A lot of people say like, 'Oh, well, I want to save that for my my paid thing. I want to say and it, you know, that.' I think that's always shooting yourself in the foot because if you have the opportunity to do something that other people don't do when you're creating content, people will watch your channel more."   [28:26]  "And that was the biggest thing with me was I took a complicated style of trading that was popular, but it had a lot of nuance that people didn't talk about or it had a lot of just what-ifs. But people always want to give it give a too high level of an explanation that doesn't really provide anybody any value."   [28:45]  "So, if you don't provide people value, then they have no reason to actually build a lasting connection with you."   [28:52]  "Now, this is where you're going to incubate future buyers because you're going to give people tangible steps to doing whatever the thing is and they're actually going to go out and be able to get value from it."   [29:04]  "So, for me, it's easy with trading. You teach people a good strategy and you tell them exactly the steps on how to do it. And if you know it works and they actually follow every step, there is a high chance they're going to come back and spend money with you because you made them money."   [29:22]  "Rather as you know if you just tell them some overall concept or strategy that doesn't really get them anywhere. So it's very very important to give tangible value."   [29:32]  "And you always want to go back and close um back in the friend archetype. This is where you can do CTAs at the end of a video. Um, you know, kind of congratulate them on watching the video, ask them to subscribe, and you you come out of that uh kind of content mind."   [29:48]  "And something else that I want to hit on, a lot of people, like we're going to watch an old video of mine that's going to be really cringeworthy here in a second, but whenever you try to make content, how many of you guys just go into complete like TV mode? Like you go into character almost."   [30:05]  "Don't fucking do that cuz people hate watching that. I hate who you hate watching when you look at it. You know what I mean? Um, if you go into that super exaggerated way of talking, it's just not authentic and people people are fucking sick of that. It's it's it's everywhere online. So, don't do that."   [30:24] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "CTA Placement Strategy"   NÚMEROS:  30% das vendas vieram de funnels contextuais semanais   VISUAL:  Diagrama mostrando posicionamento estratégico de CTAs: Soft CTA após Friend/Storytelling, Hard CTA após What/antes do How, Final CTA no encerramento   [30:27]  "So, now with CTAs, you can put them in a couple areas. Now, this is very important if you're actually trying to get an outcome, whether it's a subscription, whether it's somebody commenting on the video, whether it's someone clicking a link to go to your funnel, whatever it is. Um, very important that you do this in the right way."   [30:46]  "So, a soft CTA to something totally free that provides a ton of value with genuinely no asks. And I don't mean giving someone a lead magnet and then having a setter blow them up to buy your thing. That's that's still an ask."   [31:01]  "But like a subscribe, you know, subscribe to the channel. Um, things of that nature. You want to put those right after you have gotten done with storytelling, right after you added the friend archetype because that's where you've built some connection with people."   [31:17]  "You've broken the ice. People feel a little bit more, you know, cool with you there. You know, you don't want to ever start the video off and just ask people to subscribe immediately. You ever see that? Does it ever work? You ever just just click the subscribe?"   [31:32]  "Maybe sometimes, but less people will click it than if you actually built a connection with them."   [31:38]  "Now, for selling, you know, directly like click my uh click the link to get a lead magnet. Um, preferably not just directly pushing to a VSL. Um, but some kind of actual value you want to put after the what before you teach them how to do it."   [31:56]  "And you want to be layering in through each of these steps that the next step is still important. So in the why, you're telling them why the what is important. Now, you're going to tell them while you're delivering the core value that you're about to teach them how to actually use this and hit on the fact that most content they're watching doesn't give them any steps on how to actually utilize it."   [32:20]  "But before you get into that next step, you can give them a lead magnet. And you want to make it contextual to whatever content you're making."   [32:28]  "So for a lot of the time where we were generating all those leads and all those sales, I had my team making a new funnel, just duplicating a funnel with a new lead magnet every single week contextual to the video."   [32:42]  "Because it's actually it's not like here, join my free course, right? It's, oh, I'm teaching you how to do uh this strategy. If you want the mirrorboard I used in this video along with a full set of steps, go ahead and click the link."   [32:57]  "A lot more people are going to click that than if you're just trying to give them some nonsense that's not contextual. So, we made a whole new funnel every single week. And about 30% of our sales came from those funnels combined. So, huge deal."   [33:13]  "Now, at the end of the video, you can throw another CTA. This one you can just completely directly solicit because people that watch all the way to the end of the video, they have much higher intent than anybody else, right?"   [33:27]  "All of the sales metrics we have are much higher on people that clicked on the funnels from the complete end of the video."   [33:34] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "VOICE - Different Archetypes"   NÚMEROS:  4 voice archetypes principais   VISUAL:  Comparação entre Motivator, Educator, Coach, e Friend - características de cada estilo de voz   [33:37]  "Now, let's talk about different voices you're going to use because this one is very, very important and most people just use one voice and that is going into character, right? They just, 'Hey guys, you know, this is welcome to my my channel and and you just sound fake.'"   [33:57]  "So, with voice, you want to have a couple different ones and you're going to use them at different sections of the video."   [34:04]  "So, the motivator, think like Tony Robbins, but not that fucking extreme, okay? Like there's a difference between going all out with this and doing it in a way that actually works."   [34:16]  "So whenever you're in the why, like if you're telling people why something's important, you want to make sure that you're varying your volume. You're kind of talking in one tone instead of breaking your words apart like that."   [34:30]  "So this one you want to have a little bit higher volume. You want to vary from high speed to medium speed because it's going to get people paying attention to more of what you're doing rather than whenever you're talking in a educator role, which this one is just going to be for the what right now."   [34:47]  "This basically is just very slow. You don't want to talk too fast because people that are watching your video, they have no idea what they're doing when it comes to the thing you're teaching them most likely."   [35:00]  "So, you want to talk very slow so they're able to actually pay attention to it. Because I know in my early content, I I talk fast as a habit. And that was something I had to work on was I would just talk fast the whole time."   [35:14]  "Now, most of you guys probably are going to lean into one of these more than the other. And to start, you want to just branch out at one at a time. Don't try to just perfect all of these cuz you don't have to be a master at all this. It just helps a ton with retention."   [35:30]  "Um, so the coach, now the coach, you want to talk with a little bit more of a sharp way of of saying things. It's pretty much the opposite of the motivator. So you want to just talk very directly when you're telling people on how to do things."   [35:45]  "And you can shift from the educator where you're talking very slow to a coach where you're telling people exactly what to do. This is how Jeremy talks most of the time. Jeremy's very good at this. He's good at drilling into you, right?"   [35:59]  "Like whenever he's over here going hard, like that's the coach because it gets your attention. Like when he was talking to you, it was just like everyone's like, 'Oh fuck he's he's fucking,' you know what I mean?"   [36:12]  "It's a lot different than when at the beginning when he's talking about the Fresh and Fit podcast and that's kind of more of like a motivator. It's kind of getting you bought in on this idea of upping your offer of making a new offer that has incentives and you're listening and it's motivating you to think everybody's getting out their phone."   [36:32]  "Everybody's getting out their their pens and pad and thinking about different ways they're going to, you know, structure their offer. I mean, I was sitting there on my phone thinking like, okay, this is awesome. And that's how that will work."   [36:44]  "And then the coach will drill it into your mind and get your attention and kind of reignite your attention because by this part of the video, you've already lost a lot of people."   [36:54]  "Now, the friend is just don't even think about this one. Just talk normal. Like, you just want to talk how you would talk to somebody while you know you're at the networking part of this event. Just be genuine like that."   [37:08]  "And I think that for most of you guys, if you're just starting, this is the only one you should really try to speak in because you'll still speak in another one of the the voice tones because it's very hard to just be genuine and normal."   [37:22]  "Like that's something you're very good at, Mason. Whenever you're in your content, you talk pretty much exactly how you talk and you know, normally. It's not all um exaggerated or anything. And that's why it connects with people."   [37:36] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Voice Tips & Techniques"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Dicas práticas: pausar em pontos importantes, falar com o rosto (expressões), variar cadência e ritmo   [37:39]  "So with voice again, um some important notes. You want to pause whenever you're making a very big point in a video. If you're able to just get good at pausing at the most important points, especially whenever you're going over the core value or coaching them how to do it, it will resonate in someone's mind and hold their attention a lot differently than if you just go over the video."   [38:05]  "Because when you create the content and when you talk to people, you know the most important things of what you're telling them. But a lot of times when people watch the video, it's hard for them to fish out what is really the the the big payoff here if you don't do this."   [38:21]  "So, another little tip uh is to talk with your face. Now, I know it sounds weird, but if you are just smiling when you talk, you're going to sound happier. Whatever expression you're making."   [38:34]  "And you could go test this. Obviously, I'm not going to test for you guys live, but you guys can test this um and and record yourself and see how different you talk whenever you have different expressions on your face."   [38:46]  "Now, of course, you're not going to um do this to an extreme point while you're making content, but it's just a cool way to kind of train yourself to have different ways of talking when you're making content."   [38:58]  "You also always want to make sure to vary your cadence and just the rhythm of of the way you're talking. You don't want to talk at one speed because again in YouTube or any kind of content the number one challenge is just getting people to actually pay attention because everybody is so brain-fried from content at this point and the people who aren't really don't give a fuck about what you're saying most of the time unless you make them give a fuck."   [39:24]  "So that's that's another thing that you can do to help out with that."   [39:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "STORYTELLING - The Power of Stories"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Ênfase em storytelling como ferramenta de engajamento emocional, contraste entre fatos secos vs narrativas envolventes   [39:31]  "All right, I'm going to tell you guys a story. No, I'm just kidding. I'm not going to do that. But um earlier with the door to door that was a small example of a story and then going from that to making $4.7 million um or generating $4.7 million in a year. It's a pretty cool story, right?"   [39:51]  "It got at least some of your guys' attention. You're like, 'Okay, maybe this guy is somebody worth listening to if he did this all organically.' And you guys have tons of stories you can tell just like this."   [40:03]  "Now, they don't have to be extreme. It can be very simple. In fact, sometimes a story should be very, very relatable. I'm like, if I was in a room of people that, you know, weren't a bunch of rich fucks like you guys, I probably wouldn't start off with something like that. You'd go a little bit more into the journey of when you you know, and I was broke, right? Cuz it's going to connect more."   [40:27]  "Now, storytelling is one of the it's it's kind of an ambiguous thing. Like, everybody thinks of storytelling, you think of like a bedtime story, right? Or like something like some cornball shit."   [40:39]  "But it's really a very important skill. Whether you're doing sales, whether you're doing content marketing, and and a lot of you guys know all this already if you guys are doing email or or any kind of marketing really, but storytelling is powerful because stories automatically will engage people at an emotional level."   [40:59]  "And it's going to hold their attention a lot more than other things you can do. No matter how painful your hook is, no matter if you just say, you know, um, oh, if you don't do this, you're going to fail. People are like, okay, I've heard that a thousand fucking times."   [41:16]  "But if you start off with a story that actually engages somebody, you now have somebody's attention a lot more than any random hook you're going to be using."   [41:26] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Story Comparison Example"   NÚMEROS:  11 million children food insecure (USDA estimate)   VISUAL:  Comparação lado a lado: Estatísticas secas vs narrativa emocional sobre Olivia, demonstrando impacto diferente   [41:29]  "So let's just compare this. So according to the USDA, an estimated 11 million children in the United States are what's known as food insecure. It means that those households there aren't enough or in those households, there isn't enough food on a regular basis for every family member to lead a healthy life. This can lead to serious health problems, poor performance in school, and emotional issues."   [41:56]  "That was fucking horrible, wasn't it? That sucked, right? Nobody's going to like you just that went in one ear and out the other. You guys probably couldn't even repeat anything I just said at all, right? I can't even repeat it, right?"   [42:10]  "But if you were to read a story more like when Olivia goes to school in the morning, her stomach is grumbling and she finds it hard to concentrate in class because she's painfully shy. She can't even tell anybody about these problems because her family has no food and she can't even eat. She's ashamed and embarrassed."   [42:30]  "Her mom picks her up and gives her an extra hard hug at the bus stop each morning as to say, 'I'm so sorry. This is going to get better.' Right? It got pretty dark there. It's like, damn, that's pretty fucked up. Right?"   [42:44]  "That's just one example. But whenever you invoke emotion, whenever you say something that people actually can connect to or have empathy to, you will literally trigger a chemical reaction in their brain where you're going to be able to have their attention a lot more than if you're just talking about some boring stuff that they don't care about or just statistics or facts because a lot of people just speak in facts all the time."   [43:11]  "And speaking in facts, you know, for some settings is appropriate and you want to speak in facts after you've got people's attention. But in order to get their attention, you need to engage them on an emotional level. And storytelling is a great way to do that."   [43:26] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Benefits of Good Storytelling"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Lista de benefícios: Engaja emoção automaticamente, Dispara empatia, Constrói influência, Cria memorabilidade   [43:29]  "So good stories automatically engage emotion. It's not it's not a very forced thing. You don't want to get too exaggerated with this. Like I know when I first started learning how to do this, I would come up with the corniest stupid you start thinking of like the craziest dumb shit."   [43:46]  "And then you you realize it's it's harder than you think to come up with good stories. But good stories automatically engage emotion. They trigger empathy. Now empathy doesn't mean somebody necessarily feeling, you know, sorry for you or something. They just just feeling for you, right?"   [44:05]  "Whatever story you had, they they can it triggers some kind of relatable feeling they've had, some issue or some situation they've been through."   [44:14]  "It also builds influence because if you can tell a story specifically in sales about where you were at in their exact same position and how you came out of it using whatever you're using, they're more likely to listen to you than if you had, you know, not been through anything they've been through. You're going to have more influence."   [44:34]  "So without storytelling, nobody's going to remember you. Your content is going to be like a boring lecture. It's going to feel like you're at school and people are going to leave before you can even say something stupid like like and subscribe, right? Like that."   [44:49]  "That's that's you're not even going to have people to that point of the video. And also never say that because it sounds ridiculous. You sound like everybody else."   [44:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Storytelling Techniques"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Técnicas práticas: Usar linking, Contar histórias relacionáveis, Usar os 5 sentidos (visual, auditivo, tátil, olfativo)   [45:01]  "So with storytelling, you can use linking. So, you can use a lot of different examples of this, but you can draw you you can list out a lot of different things that you've been um through in your life or things in your business or when you're building your product. And you can link any of those things to whatever topic you're talking about in your content."   [45:22]  "Whatever it is, whether it's sales, whether it's running ads, whether it's starting a business, you can link anything together using that concept."   [45:30]  "You always want to make sure to tell relatable stories. And if you're like for me today, if I'm um talking to you guys about trading and I talk just about trading, it would only connect with a very small amount of you guys, right? So, you have to tell stories that are going to connect to the person you're talking to."   [45:49]  "You always want to make sure to use the senses. So you want to talk about things that are visual, things that you hear, things that you feel, smell, and it can be very basic stuff like a story that I have tested in a lot of different content that's trading content is I talk about whenever I lost a shitload of money."   [46:09]  "I remember sitting there and the screen was the only light in the room. It was very late at night. I drink coffee all the time. So my office always smelled like coffee and my eyes literally burned from just staring at trading screens all the time. And I was always stressed out. Right?"   [46:28]  "That that story performed as a hook much better than anything else we use because it used all of the senses and not only just for retention but also for revenue because we track every video with Hyros because you're connecting on an emotional level with people."   [46:46] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Finding Your Stories"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Prompts para descobrir histórias pessoais: Como o produto surgiu? Qual o backstory? O que estava acontecendo na sua vida? Aprendeu com outros?   [46:49]  "Now a lot of you guys are going to sit here and say like I don't have any stories right or you think of stories you're like what are you going to tell like your fucking life story or something? You know what I mean? Like nobody wants to to listen to that. But you have a ton of stories whether you realize it or not."   [47:07]  "How did your product become uh or how did your product or service come to be? What is the backstory behind that? What was going on in your life whenever you started that? Did you learn the solution from somebody else? Did you learn from other people? There's there's tons of endless stories that you can you can come up with or maybe you know you you were trying to you weren't trying to solve a problem and it just happened randomly."   [47:33]  "Like for me for example the way that I started streaming when I was trading I joined a trading community myself a low ticket community and I had been trading for a couple years before it was really popular. So I joined this group and that was during COVID whenever it was just now getting popular and I had been trading for a couple years."   [47:53]  "So whenever I started posting trades all the time and I was making good money people naturally were like oh like how are you doing this? Can you teach me? So then I started to go on live on Discord. This is back. How many of you guys do you guys even know what Discord? A lot of you guys probably do, right? It's more Every trader loves Discord."   [48:14]  "Um, but at that time, you could only have 50 people on a live stream in Discord. So, the only reason I even made a YouTube ever was because whenever we were in that live stream and I was trading with everybody, I ended up just going making a YouTube so people could just watch the video. I had no plans at all of being a content creator or selling anything online whatsoever."   [48:37]  "So, that's a pretty cool story, right? So, you have a ton of stories probably just as similar or better than that yourself. You just got to look for them."   [48:46] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "FINDING TOPICS - The Right Way"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Estratégia de identificação de tópicos: Não reinventar a roda, Estudar competidores, Usar VidIQ, Rastrear outliers   [48:49]  "Now, when it comes to topics, this is the most important part of YouTube because almost everybody that tries to come up with video ideas for YouTube does it completely wrong. Everybody wants to be a pioneer. Everybody wants to reinvent the wheel."   [49:04]  "So, don't try to reinvent the wheel. You don't want to just think about random ideas that haven't worked. It's kind of like who is doing SaaS. So you I'm not even an expert in SaaS, but in SaaS isn't one of the metas to kind of like find a product um something that's already proven and then make it better."   [49:25]  "It's a lot easier and a lot less risky than just trying to come with an idea that you think's cool but other people don't really think is cool. Same thing with YouTube."   [49:35]  "Because if you just come up with random ideas, if nobody else has came up with it yet, it probably sucks. There's probably a reason nobody's came up with it yet, right? Um there are, you know, some outliers with that."   [49:49]  "But now, you want to study your competitors. You want to make sure to always be very vigilant of the people that are doing better in your area and what they're doing and how they're doing it different than you. You want to make sure that you use a tool to do this. And you want to track their outliers."   [50:06] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "VidIQ Tool Demo"   NÚMEROS:  $20-30/mês, Multiplicadores tipo 12x, 10x mostrados   VISUAL:  Screenshot do VidIQ mostrando plugin do Chrome com métricas de performance de vídeos, outliers destacados com multiplicadores   [50:09]  "And this is going to be done using something called VidIQ. So, on VidIQ, it'll do you install it as a Chrome plugin, and you can't really see it, but there's these little um 12x, 10x. It'll basically show you different videos and how much more they perform compared to the average video on that channel."   [50:28]  "So, you just want to go across a list of competitors from your niche with this plugin in and find all the videos or the videos that worked well for them and then just re-iterate them for yourself."   [50:40]  "Now, a lot of people think that's like stealing content, but you're going to come up with a different topic. You're going to come up with a different core value than they did. And never just copy a video word for word. Just copy the idea."   [50:54]  "So, VidIQ is the tool. It's super cheap. It's like 20 or $30 a month, something like that. Um, but this is how you're going to track your competitors."   [51:04] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Topic Strategy Summary"   NÚMEROS:  5-10 competidores recomendados, 5 ideias por semana   VISUAL:  Framework de estratégia de tópicos: Identificar 5-10 competidores → Usar VidIQ para outliers → Buscar padrões → Adaptar de outros nichos → Gerar 5 ideias/semana   [51:07]  "So, kind of high level overview of topics. You just want to make sure you're always aware of competitors. I'd pick five to 10. That's more than enough. Use VidIQ to identify the outliers and patterns on their channels."   [51:20]  "Then you want to go into other niches and you can kind of find ideas as well. Like for example, Hormozi does a lot of of this. Like he'll say, you know, 68 minutes of business advice in whatever minutes or, you know, or or 10 years of business advice in 68 minutes, whatever it is. You can take those ideas as long as they can, you know, kind of plug and play with your niche."   [51:43]  "But if you're going to do YouTube, always make sure to come up with five ideas per week. And there's no way you don't get at least one good video out of that."   [51:52] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO  ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "YouTube Strategy Recap"   NÚMEROS:  5 elementos principais   VISUAL:  Resumo visual dos 5 componentes: Linking, Structuring, Voice, Storytelling, Finding Topics   [51:55]  "So, to wrap up things with YouTube, you're going to want to make sure that you understand linking, structuring your videos so people actually watch them, using your voice properly, learning how to tell stories so you connect with people, and learning how to find topics so people will actually watch the content you're making."   [52:15]  "Do any of you guys have any questions on YouTube? How many of you guys are actually gonna gonna start a YouTube?"   [52:22]  "I already have. You already have one. Okay, cool. So, do you guys have any questions at all on YouTube or growing from an organic standpoint?"   [52:32]  "Uh post frequency, like how are you thinking about quality versus volume?"   [52:37]  "So, great question. Um we have I've tested out posting once, twice, and three times a week. I think this is going to be subjective for everyone. For me personally, posting three times becomes a bit productized in the sense that you start just posting to post."   [52:55]  "And I always say with anything um but definitely with content, quality over quantity. Don't post five times a day. Everybody says like, 'Oh, let's post five times a day. You're going to' No, stupid. Don't do that. Um if you can't post anything of quality, it's actually better to not post anything at all."   [53:14]  "So that would be I mean, I'd say start with one video if you could. Everybody can do one a week."   [53:20]  "So my uh YouTube channel is linked to my podcast and so we publish two to three podcast episodes a week and what I'm trying to understand is that is it better for me to post uh videos for my audience based on certain needs that they have on that same channel or should it be a completely different channel?"   [53:40]  "Completely different channel for sure. Yeah, podcast should always be a completely different channel. Yeah. And you can like link it in the bottom. It'll have like linked channels, not like the podcast playlist thing, but you can actually link the channel and then you'll get the traffic from your main channel."   [53:57]  "But I always tell people, even if you have like you're trying to have a business channel, I was just telling you this earlier, always have a personal brand one like on YouTube. People are going to always drive to that more and just filter that into whatever the thing is, the podcast or whatever."   [54:15]  "So I have the personal one, but it's old, you know, uh it's got I think like maybe maybe about 2,000 followers. So it's not a lot. Um, but that personal one was like a lot of just like I just keep that, keep those just start adding new ones and just do it that way. Start from scratch."   [54:35]  "I mean, how many subscribers does it have? I would just start from scratch. Yeah, because at the end of the like YouTube is very heavy on content that your audience wants to watch."   [54:47]  "So, if you're posting stuff that your your audience doesn't want to watch, it'll automatically kill your video's chance of success. So if you have book reviews and you start posting business stuff, none of those people are probably going to watch it and it won't show it to other people. So just start from scratch."   [55:06]  "So right now with my mastermind offer, my coaching offer, it's like my ideal plan is like 10 to 30k a month of helping them double. But I think you kind of answered my question like is it worth creating YouTube for that audience or should I just really focus on my personal brand entrepreneurship and then funnel them into the high ticket?"   [55:27]  "Yeah. No, great question. I would just do with your personal brand. Like look at Hormozi for example. I mean once you build a big enough personal brand, you can pretty much do anything in that's relative to what you've been talking about and even more, right?"   [55:42]  "And then and again it's like the credibility thing. Like when I found Jeremy, I immediately looked at his page was like, 'Oh, I fucking like this guy. This guy's fucking going hard. Cool. I'll buy his shit.' I'm a low info buyer. I I value it like what's the risk here? Does, you know, is this going to resonate with me? And now here I am."   [56:03]  "So I think that that that's going to be valuable for that as well. The people that are going to look at your Instagram, kind of see about you. Um, look into your YouTube because people do their research, especially clients like that."   [56:17]  "So, start with personal brand storytelling, like make it about my journey and what I'm doing and then, uh, then follow them. Exactly. Products. Exactly. 100%."   [56:28]  "Similar question. I have a personal channel that's got 20,000 followers and then a business channel that has about 5,000 followers. The business channel doesn't get many views or much engagement, but it's very specific to our business. Um, can we do duplicate content where you put the same video on the personal channel and the business channel? Should we only put the niche content on the business channel or only on the personal channel?"   [56:54]  "I would separate it for sure, 100%. Um, for one, if you actually post the same video file on um the same platform, it'll trigger the algorithm to to nerf it because people are doing all the fan page stuff. So like even when you're doing the fan pages that's on, you know, other platforms, you want to make sure it's a different file if you're going to do that."   [57:18]  "Now, number two, with YouTube, it's very much like the reason the topic section is so important is because what you're talking about has to be what that person wants to watch. And it's very unlikely that the same that's going to be the same for both of those channels. You know what I mean?"     📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO   NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS: $4.7 milhões em receita em 1 ano $11,989 gastos totais em ads ($6,167 YouTube + $5,822 Facebook) $40,000 recebidos do YouTube em revenue share 2,600 → 177,000 subscribers em 1 ano e 9 meses 5,000-6,000 leads orgânicos por mês <1% taxa de chargeback/refund 70% dos leads via YouTube 30% das vendas de funnels contextuais semanais 2-3 vendas geradas de ads pagos VidIQ: $20-30/mês   ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS: 4P Flywheel: Presence, Platform, (implícitos: Product, People) Framework de estrutura de vídeo: Friend → Why → What → How → Friend 3 posicionamentos de CTA: Soft após Friend, Hard após What, Final no encerramento Funnels contextuais semanais (novo lead magnet por vídeo) Build in public strategy (autenticidade > perfeição) Tracking com Hyros para métricas de revenue por vídeo VidIQ para identificar outliers de competidores (12x, 10x multiplicadores)   ESTRUTURA: 5 Componentes de Platform: Linking, Structuring, Voice, Storytelling, Finding Topics 4 Voice Archetypes: Motivator (Tony Robbins style), Educator (slow/clear), Coach (sharp/direct), Friend (natural) 3 Pilares de Marketing: Awareness, Nurture, Trust Linking: conexões indiretas entre ideias não relacionadas Storytelling: usar 5 sentidos (visual, auditivo, tátil, olfativo) Topics: 5-10 competidores, 5 ideias/semana, não reinventar a roda   DADOS FINANCEIROS: ROI: $4.7M receita / $11,989 investimento = 392x retorno Revenue share YouTube > investimento em ads (net positive) High ticket sales em indústria difícil (trading/info) Métricas de conversão mais altas em viewers que assistem até o final Vídeos técnicos convertem melhor que vídeos de alto subscriber count Frequência de postagem testada: 1x, 2x, 3x/semana (2x recomendado)   DURAÇÃO:  57:30  FORMATO:  Apresentação de mastermind com Q&A ao vivo
